question: You've been planning your project using a waterfall methodology. The project team have collected the requirements from the customer who is eager to begin work as soon as possible. You set a meeting with the business analysts in your team to create the scope statement based on these requirements. What will you do next?

options.A: Include the scope description, deliverables, acceptance criteria, and exclusions
options.B: Include the scope description, work breakdown structure, and work breakdown structure dictionary
options.C: Include the scope management plan, statement of work, and requirements traceability Matrix
options.D: Include the project Charter, project management plan, and accepted deliverables

explanation: The project scope statement includes the scope description, deliverables, acceptance criteria, and any exclusions; do not confuse this with the scope baseline which includes the scope description, WBS, WBS dictionary, and work packages. Page 154 under the project scope statement in the PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition.

question: You've been assigned to a project that is behind schedule. Some of your team approach you and ask you what they should be working on. You check with your team and one of your senior analysts recommends you work together to create the work breakdown structure dictionary. What will you do next?

options.A: Create a scope breakdown with unique identifier, description, and acceptance criteria
options.B: Work with the senior analyst to create a resource assignment Matrix instead and coach them on the difference between a resource assignment Matrix and work breakdown structure dictionary
options.C: Gather the project scope and break it down into smaller work packages
options.D: Create a scope breakdown with unique identifier, description, and resource requirements

explanation: A resource assignment Matrix is the best approach, showing the project resources assigned to each work package and illustrating the connections between work packages and project team members, which answers what the team should be working on. Page 317 for the resource assignment Matrix in the PMBOK Guide.

question: The project Charter has been approved and you've identified the stakeholders you'll be working with and who will be impacted as the project progresses. You notice multiple stakeholders who seem to be actively trying to sabotage your project and your efforts. What will you do next?

options.A: Review the stakeholder engagement assessment Matrix and invite feedback from those who are resistant
options.B: Remove the people who seem to be slowing your project from your project team
options.C: Escalate the problem to their functional managers for disciplinary action
options.D: Review the impact over influence Matrix to determine how to engage with the stakeholders

explanation: Assessing stakeholder engagement and gathering their feedback is the best answer; this question involves determining which stakeholders are at risk and then using communication, interpersonal, and team skills to engage them. Page 521 and 527 under manage stakeholder engagement communication skills in the PMBOK Guide.

question: Your project involves nearly a dozen teams who are dispersed around the country at different sites with thousands of impacted stakeholders. The executives in these areas are concerned with the changes the project will bring and are starting to push back on your planning efforts. What will you do next?

options.A: Create the project Charter and arrange for sign off by the executives
options.B: Create the communications management plan to ensure that their communication needs are met
options.C: Raise a change request for the project to arrange to co-locate all the teams
options.D: Create the requirements traceability Matrix so you can trace the executive's requirements back to the scope you deliver and keep them engaged

explanation: The communications management plan contains stakeholder communication requirements and is the current plan for collecting, creating, and distributing information in a timely manner; it identifies team members, stakeholders, and the work involved in the communications process. Page 377 communications management plan in the PMBOK Guide.

question: A number of risks have been raised on your project. You perform qualitative analysis to get a subjective risk rating but your project customer is not satisfied. They would like you to perform additional qualitative risk analysis beyond impact and likelihood. What will you analyze next?

options.A: Simulation, sensitivity analysis, and decision tree analysis
options.B: Cost of failure, cost of appraisal, and cost of prevention
options.C: Executive needs, political needs, and promotion needs
options.D: Urgency, proximity, and strategic impact of the risks

explanation: As part of qualitative risk analysis, other risk parameters you might measure include urgency, proximity, dormancy, manageability, controllability, detectability, strategic impact, and propinquity. Page 423-424 under qualitative risk analysis assessment of other parameters in the PMBOK Guide.

question: One of the risks on your project is significantly large and could seriously impact your company if it ever came about. It has captured the attention of high-ranking executives who have each proposed a response and think that theirs is the best. You decide to solve the argument with a decision tree. What will you do next?

options.A: Rate the risks for their probability and impact and get a rating for each
options.B: Note each of the options on a vertical bar chart for their impact sensitivity on cost
options.C: Note the various response options with their cost impact and their probability of occurrence
options.D: Perform a simulation in depth of all the responses against multiple criteria to test their impact

explanation: A decision tree shows various options with their benefit or cost and various often controllable outcomes with their probability of occurrence, allowing you to choose the path with the least cost and the least probability of impact. Page 435 under decision tree analysis in the PMBOK Guide.

question: You've recently become a project manager for a large organization and you're not sure what to expect. Your colleague tells you that the organization is large enough to have a substantial project management office and it is a directing PMO. What will you do next?

options.A: Collaborate with the other project managers in the PMO to find a project that suits your skill set
options.B: Get started on a business case in your work area to initiate a project
options.C: Await being assigned to a project by the PMO
options.D: Review the project management frameworks, templates, and tools mandated for use by the PMO

explanation: There are three types of PMOs: supportive (templates and guidelines), controlling (adherence to frameworks and templates), and directive (high degree of control where they directly manage the projects and assign project managers). Page 48 under project management office in the PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition.

question: The project customer approaches you as they are worried that the project is not delivering fast enough. You review the project schedule and cost so you can see how the project is tracking. The actual cost is $648,000, the earned value is $601,000, and the planned value is $629,000. Which best describes the project status?

options.A: Ahead of schedule and under budget
options.B: Ahead of schedule and over budget
options.C: Behind schedule and over budget
options.D: Behind schedule and under budget

explanation: The schedule variance (EV - PV = 601k - 629k = -28k) shows the project is behind schedule, and the cost variance (EV - AC = 601k - 648k = -47k) shows it is over budget. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 261 under Earned Value Analysis.

question: The people and resources involved in your project are currently costing a large amount of money and your project sponsor comments about it frequently. You check how the project is tracking. The planned value is $840,000, the earned value is $795,000, and the actual cost is $755,000. What will you do next?

options.A: Utilize the project contingency reserves
options.B: Raise a change request to reduce the project scope and bring the cost back on track
options.C: Reduce the project resources to bring the project cost back on track
options.D: Add more resources to bring the project schedule back on track

explanation: The project is behind schedule (EV - PV = 795k - 840k = -45k) but under budget (EV - AC = 795k - 755k = +40k), so adding more resources with the available budget will bring the schedule back on track. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 262 under Variance Analysis.

question: Team members from parts of the organization have been assigned to your project at an agreed level of 50% availability. As the project progresses you notice that they are being pulled back into their normal roles. You also notice that your project is falling behind schedule. What will you do next?

options.A: Talk with the project steering committee and arrange for more resources to assist your project
options.B: Talk with the functional managers and negotiate clear and written assignments with reliable scheduling priorities
options.C: Raise a risk to project delivery and assign it to the functional managers
options.D: Raise an issue in the issue log and raise it at the next project working group

explanation: The best approach is to directly and collaboratively negotiate with the functional managers, establishing clear written assignments and reliable scheduling priorities before escalating. See PMBOK 6th Edition pages 332-333 under Interpersonal and Team Skills: Negotiation.

question: You have been planning a project with the manager of a functional area who needs to increase their profit by 57% in the next three years. You are helping him put together a business case. He has come up with 18 different project ideas to reach the goal but that will take approximately seven years. The manager pushes you to find a way to do them all in three years. What will you do next?

options.A: Prioritize the ideas based on impact and cost and note the required, desired, or optional next to each of the projects in the business case
options.B: Advise the manager that good things take time to deliver
options.C: Add the appropriate amount of cost for extra resources to your project plan to get the work done
options.D: Set a meeting with the functional area experts and ask them to pick the top ideas for delivery

explanation: The project business case includes analysis of the situation with decision criteria where courses of action may be assessed as required, desired, or optional, helping to prioritize by impact and cost. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 31 under Project Business Case.

question: Your project Charter has been approved and your stakeholder analysis reveals multiple impacted highly paid executives with the power to influence your career. As you approach some of these stakeholders they raise their concerns about the project, how it is progressing, and what it involves. What will you do next?

options.A: Perform an impact over influence analysis on all your stakeholders
options.B: Create your project management plan and review its detail regularly with the impacted or influential stakeholders
options.C: Circulate the signed project Charter to anyone who needs convincing that the project is needed
options.D: Reduce your communication with most stakeholders as they cannot resist what they don't know about

explanation: Developing the project management plan and reviewing it regularly with stakeholders assists with their engagement; this step logically follows the approved project Charter and stakeholder identification. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 82 under Develop Project Management Plan.

question: You have just created your project management plan and are reviewing it with your stakeholders. It includes the subsidiary plans of scope management, schedule management, cost management, resources, and quality. Some of your stakeholders raise concerns that they cannot see what needs to be baselined or how to measure the project performance and changes. What will you do next?

options.A: Advise your stakeholders the information they need is in the subsidiary plans
options.B: Meet as often as needed with your project stakeholders to increase their engagement and acceptance of your project
options.C: Show them the project budget timeline with costs projected on a schedule
options.D: Create the configuration management plan, change management plan, and performance measurement baseline with your team and include them in your project management plan

explanation: The configuration management plan defines what needs to be baselined, the change management plan describes how to process changes, and the performance measurement baseline is the integrated scope-schedule-cost plan used to measure project performance. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 88 under Project Management Plan Additional Components.

question: Part of your project work needs to be outsourced to a supplier. You are asked to ensure that the cost for the outsourced portion of the project does not exceed $1.2 million, and if the work comes in under cost and ahead of schedule you may provide additional money to the supplier. What sort of contract will you work with your procurement office to prepare?

options.A: Create a firm fixed price contract for the seller
options.B: Create a fixed price incentive fee contract for the seller
options.C: Create a Cost Plus fixed fee contract
options.D: Create a Cost Plus Award fee contract

explanation: A fixed price incentive fee contract includes financial incentives tied to agreed-upon metrics and sets a price ceiling above which all costs are the seller's responsibility, matching the requirement for a cost ceiling plus a performance bonus. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 471 under Contract Types.

question: You take the completed scope to your project sponsor for them to accept. The project sponsor advises she cannot accept the deliverables as she does not know the outcomes of testing and whether the scope met her requirements or needs further changes. What will you do next?

options.A: Set a meeting with the project sponsor directly to address their concerns
options.B: Review the requirements traceability matrix and show the sponsor how each requirement is met by project scope
options.C: Review the quality report together with your testing leader and note which items may need change requests if any
options.D: Review the change requests for the project to show the sponsor whether any corrective actions needed to be made

explanation: The quality report includes corrective action recommendations, defect and rework summaries, and findings from the control quality process, making it the ideal document to show the sponsor the state of the deliverables before acceptance. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 296 under Quality Reports.

question: Multiple unforeseen events have happened that have set your project behind schedule and in trying to bring it back on track you have spent a considerable amount of money. Now the project sponsor has threatened to stop the project unless they can see how much money the project will cost by the time it is finished. What will you do next?

options.A: Perform a reserve analysis on the remaining contingency and management reserves
options.B: Create a project estimate at completion and compare it to the budget at completion
options.C: Create the to-complete performance index with your team to meet your project goal
options.D: Review the cost performance index to see how the project cost is tracking

explanation: As the project progresses the team develops a forecast for the estimate at completion (EAC), which may differ from the original budget at completion (BAC) based on project performance, providing the sponsor a revised total cost figure. See PMBOK 6th Edition pages 264 and 269 under Forecasting and Control Costs: Cost Forecasts.

question: Your project plan includes an estimate of how long the project will take via a project schedule, how much it will cost, and the resources that will be required. The project sponsor believes the project can be done with around 34% fewer resources. What will you do next?

options.A: Dismiss the sponsor's comments as uneducated and proceed with your plan as is
options.B: Reduce the resources in your project plan so the project can meet your sponsor's expectations
options.C: Create a basis of estimates with your project team and review this with your sponsor to agree on a way forward
options.D: Raise a risk to your project delivery in the risk register if the plan goes ahead with fewer resources

explanation: The basis of estimates provides a clear and complete understanding of how estimates are derived, including assumptions, constraints, and ranges, helping the sponsor understand the reasoning and agree on the best way forward together. See PMBOK 6th Edition page 204 under Basis of Estimates.
